title: Minister eases AI burden on FWC
full_text:
Employment and Workplace Relations Minister Amanda Rishworth has unveiled a suite of changes to assist the Fair Work Commission reduce its surging workload caused by a rise in AI driven claims.
Under a bill to be introduced into federal parliament on Wednesday, she will outline changes to the commission’s processes that are designed to speed up the tribunal’s handling of claims.
Key changes include the commission being able to dispense with the requirement for costly and time-consuming hearings into jurisdictional matters before dealing with certain dismissal-related disputes.
The commission, where appropriate, will be able to determine matters on the papers without the need to proceed to a formal hearing.
Where a person’s application has been dismissed as frivolous, vexatious, or with no reasonable prospects of success, they can be prevented from making a further application.
The commission will also be able to dismiss unfair deactivation or unfair termination applications that are frivolous, vexatious or have no reasonable prospects of success.
The changes will also allow the recommencement of supported bargaining without the need for a new authorisation, where an authorisation has already been made less than two years ago, saving the commission and the parties time and money.
In a bid to speed up dismissal proceedings, commission president Adam Hatcher will be able to delegate power to certify that a dismissal-related dispute could not be resolved by the commission and can proceed straight to arbitration or to court.
Ms Rishworth said the amendments would help the FWC allocate its time and resources more efficiently so it could continue to effectively perform its vital role as the national independent workplace relations tribunal.
“This will mean individual matters can be dealt with more quickly, so workers and employers can get more timely resolutions to their issues and enable the commission to respond to challenges presented by the increase of AI and paid agents.
“Importantly, this will ensure the commission can continue to effectively perform its crucial functions, such as setting minimum wages, adjusting awards, approving enterprise agreements, and resolving disputes.
“My priority is working to ensure our reforms continue to deliver secure jobs, better pay and co-operative and productive workplaces,” Ms Rishworth said.
The bill will include a measure to enable commonwealth spending to support enterprise bargaining that promotes job security and fair conditions; and also allow the government to preference enterprise agreements negotiated in good faith and genuinely agreed, where appropriate to do so.
Small business owners in the road transport industry will also be given access to protections against unfair contracts through the commission. “Truckies are unable to access our unfair contracts protections in the commission because their high out-of-pocket costs lift them above the high-income threshold,” Ms Rishworth said. “We will fix this by creating a new high-income threshold that is fit for purpose.”
